data.title: Phoenix Education Partners: A Top Tier Play In For-Profit Education
data.description:
Lately, I have been getting more and more into the for-profit education space. This is a bit odd to me because it's an industry I frankly don't like. It's not that I don't like the economics of it. Rather, I don't like the incentive structure. I don't think that education should be a for-profit endeavor. But regardless, it doesn't matter what I think. What matters is what is fundamentally attractive for investors. And perhaps the best for-profit education firm that I have come across since digging into this industry is Phoenix Education Partners (PXED). With a market capitalization of $1.14 billion, it's not exactly a large enterprise. However, management has been growing the business rapidly in recent years. Add on top of this just how cheap shares are, and I see no reason to be anything other than incredibly bullish.

Soon, investors will have more data that will hopefully show continued growth for the company. On April 7th, management is expected to announce financial results for the second quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Because of when the company became publicly traded, we don't really have concrete data to compare it to for the previous year. But on the whole, the expectation for financial performance looks healthy. Unless something big and unexpected happens that negatively affects the picture for the business, I believe that assigning it a ‘strong buy’ rating is the appropriate course of action here.

Time to learn about Phoenix Education Partners

If you have not heard about Phoenix Education Partners before, you almost certainly have heard about its hallmark business known as The University of Phoenix. That institution was founded back in 1976 and has been continuously accredited since 1978. Unlike traditional educational institutions, The University of Phoenix is a for-profit endeavor that offers its students a wide array of educational experiences. Management claims that, in the five years of its operations ending in August of last year, it served more than 1.1 million alumni and conferred almost 1.3 million degrees. Overwhelmingly, the company focuses on those aged 18 to 22. And at the end of last year, the company offered 72 degree-granting programs and 33 non-degree certificate programs, with fields such as healthcare, the social and behavioral sciences, education, nursing, IT, business, and even more all offered. It even has certain doctoral studies programs.

70% of its enrollment is focused on bachelor's degrees. However, it would be a mistake to think of the company as a provider of nothing more than lower-tiered education. Yes, it does also have about 11% of its student body focused on associate degrees. But 16% of enrollment involves master's degrees, while 3% involves doctoral degrees. This is definitely out of sync with what we can see when looking at most educational institutions. My alma mater, Case Western Reserve University, as an example, had 12,398 people enrolled last year. Only 52.7% of those were undergraduates. Graduate and professional degrees accounted for the rest. But I digress.

Over the last few years now, management has done a really good job of growing the business. In the chart above, you can see that revenue jumped from $835.2 million to $1.02 billion from 2023 to 2025. This came as the average degree enrollment for the company grew from 71,000 to 81,900. Management boasts what it calls its Tuition Price Guarantee, which is a guarantee that the price per course that students pay is frozen at the time of his or her enrollment date. This does mean that revenue growth from enrollees that are attending the institution must come from a rise in the number of enrollees, combined with higher prices as new enrollees join, and an increase in the number of course starts per student throughout the year. This last point management indicated was a revenue driver from 2024 to 2025. But on top of this, the company enjoyed improved retention.

As revenue increases, profitability grows as well. Net income more than doubled from $64.9 million in 2023 to $133.9 million last year. On an adjusted basis, net profits skyrocketed from $105.9 million to $173 million. We did see operating cash flow fall during this time. But on an adjusted basis where we strip out changes in working capital, we get a nice jump from $168 million to $251.3 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business grew from $157.5 million to $243.9 million.

For the 2026 fiscal year, we have only had data covering one additional operating quarter. For that quarter, revenue for the company came in at $262 million. That's an increase of 2.9% over the $254.7 million that the business reported a year earlier. Predictably, this rise in sales was primarily thanks to a growth in enrollment, with that number expanding from 82,200 to 85,600. This is fantastic in and of itself, and management claimed that the firm also benefited from improved student retention. However, there were some weak spots. This included, as an example, a rise in discounts year over year as the company saw a higher percentage of its average total degreed enrollment come through employer relationships as opposed to individuals signing up.

The employer angle is particularly interesting here. Management has been making a concerted effort to invest along these lines. And that investment is certainly paying off. In their first-quarter earnings call, the management team at the business said that employer-affiliated enrollment is now responsible for 34% of total enrollment for the business. That's up from 31% a year earlier. By partnering up with employers, the company is also learning about and pushing out new product offerings that extend beyond degree programs. Presumably, this would fall under the certification category. Management is making other changes as well. For instance, it is employing AI in order to improve enrollment conversion and retention. Management emphasized the importance of using AI in order to boost skill sets for students, including through the integration of it into curriculum on top of institutional operations for the business.

This rise in revenue did boost profitability for the company in some ways, but in other ways the firm fell short. Overall net income plummeted from $46.4 million to $15.5 million. A measurable chunk of this deterioration came from increased spending on strategic alternatives, restructuring, and other initiatives. The strategic alternatives category basically involved costs associated with the company becoming publicly traded, as well as pursuing strategic ownership alternatives prior to going through its IPO last year. But then we also had a cybersecurity incident that cost the business $4.5 million. In essence, the company left some of its data exposed, leading to additional expenses and the announcement of multiple lawsuits against the business. This definitely should be monitored by investors moving forward. Still, other profitability metrics fared well. Adjusted net income, as an example, inched higher from $50.9 million to $53.6 million. Operating cash flow went from negative $34 million to positive $31.1 million. It is true that adjusted operating cash flow dropped from $78.5 million to $70.6 million. However, EBITDA for the business expanded from $70.1 million to $75.2 million.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, we should expect continued growth for the company. Management believes that revenue for the year will range between $1.025 billion and $1.035 billion. From that, the firm expects EBITDA to rise from $243.9 million to between $244 million and $249 million. This would likely translate at the midpoint to adjusted net profits of $174.8 million and to adjusted operating cash flow of $254 million.

Using these estimates, as well as the results that the company saw in 2025, we can see in the chart above how shares are priced at the moment. These are incredibly cheap shares. And in that table, you can see exactly how the firm stacks up against five other similar for-profit enterprises in the education space. Without exception, Phoenix Education Partners is the cheapest of the group. To illustrate for you exactly how cheap the stock is, I created the chart below. You can see how much upside the stock would experience if it were to trade at the multiple of the cheapest of these five comparable enterprises. In this case, upside would range between 54.5% and 291.1%. And if we use the average trading multiple of these businesses, the range would be between 253.9% and 398.7%. Even capturing half of any of these figures would be significant. When you add on top of this the fact that the business has no debt and enjoys a cash position of $203.2 million, which is equivalent to approximately 17.8% of its market capitalization, there are plenty of reasons to be bullish here.

Hopefully, when management announces results for the second quarter of the 2026 fiscal year on April 7th, we will get even more insight into the health and growth prospects of the company. Because of the data that is publicly available and the timing of the IPO of the business, we don't have any detailed figures for the second quarter of 2025. However, management is expected, according to analysts, to generate sales of about $220.8 million. Earnings per share should come in at around $0.24, which would translate to net profits of about $9.3 million. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share should total about $0.34. That would mean adjusted net profits totaling $13.2 million. Because of the absence of data for the second quarter of 2025, it is difficult to gauge just how important hitting these metrics would be. But we do know that, with the performance achieved in the first quarter of this year compared to the same time last year and the trajectory that the firm seems to be on according to management, it's likely that overall financial performance will be viewed in a favorable light.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available at this time, I would argue that Phoenix Education Partners is a fantastic prospect to consider buying into. It is an incredibly healthy company, even if it does have some challenges, such as fallout from the cybersecurity incident that it contended with. Growth is impressive, and management recently commented that regulatory threats to it are reflected in their own outlook for the business. When you consider how cheap shares are and how strong the balance sheet is, it seems to me as though it justifies a ‘strong buy’ rating at this time.

data.title: Progress Remains Elusive For Citizens & Northern
data.description:
This analysis serves as an update to my coverage of Citizens & Northern Corporation (CZNC) that was published last December. The bank holding company that operates in the states of Pennsylvania and New York was able to complete its first full quarter following the acquisition of Susquehanna Community Financial at the end of 2025. The deal was one of the smaller community bank deals from last year, with a total value just north of $44 million. However, CZNC is not exactly a banking giant itself, with a market cap of just $400 million.

I decided to revisit the company to see what, if any, impact the newly acquired assets had on the financial results from Citizens & Northern. I also wanted to see what, if any, projections could be made for the company’s first earnings report from 2026 that is due at the end of this month. In my previous analyses, I have rated CZNC as a Hold, and the stock has exceeded my expectations with double-digit gains over the last seven months. However, the environment for all regional banks has become more challenging in recent weeks with energy prices rising and the odds of a Fed rate cut seemingly getting smaller every day.

Compared to other regional banks that I have written about on this site, I still do not see a compelling reason to raise CZNC to a Buy at this time. Performance metrics for the company are improving but still below average. The company's asset quality has dipped slightly since my last article was published. The sizeable dividend may be the best reason to hold Citizens & Northern in the first half of 2026.

Company Overview

Citizens & Northern Corporation is the holding company for Citizens & Northern Bank, which provides traditional banking services for both consumer and commercial clients. The bank also offers investment products, insurance sales, and wealth management services through three smaller subsidiaries. All told, CZNC operates 35 banking and loan production offices, with a majority of those locations in northern and southeastern Pennsylvania. A map showing the company’s geographical service area is given below.

At the end of 2025, Citizens & Northern controlled $3.1 billion in total assets, total deposits of $2.6 billion, and net loans outstanding of just over $2.3 billion. All of these numbers are substantially higher than when my last analysis was written because of the addition of the operations of Susquehanna Community Financial. CZNC also possessed a debt securities portfolio valued at $507 million at the end of Q4 2025.

The table below shows a detailed breakdown of the various segments of the bank’s loan portfolio and their changes over the last five years. The addition of Susquehanna’s loan assets did little to significantly change the allocations between the loan types. The only real movement I noticed was that Citizens & Northern has gradually trimmed its exposure to residential mortgages, on a relative basis, since 2022.

The total weight of non-owner-occupied commercial real estate for Citizens & Northern Bank at the end of last year was 39.4%. When the 13.2% of owner-occupied CRE is added to the mix, over half of the total loans outstanding for the bank are tied to some form of commercial real estate. All other commercial loans have an allocation of 23.8%. Residential mortgages hold a weight of 18.9%, down from 21.5% at the end of 2024.

Of the $2.6 billion in deposits held at C&N Bank, $531 million, or 20.7%, are held in non-interest-bearing accounts. Just over $811 million of all deposits at the bank are uninsured. Almost $639 million is uninsured and uncollateralized. These latter numbers are not a sign of a problem at this time, but they could become more of an issue during a period of added stress on the bank's loan portfolio.

Income-focused investors may want to take a longer look at Citizens & Northern. The forward yield on its stock is 5.0%, even though its quarterly payout of $0.28 has not changed since Q2 2021. The company does distribute a significant share of its earnings, as its payout ratio on a GAAP basis is just over 75%. However, its five-year average for this data point is 67.25%, so the company has been maintaining this high payout rate for an extended period of time. That being said, any dividend raises appear unlikely in FY 2026.

Analysis of Citizens & Northern

Citizens & Northern has an unfortunate history of falling short of earnings expectations over the last couple of years. When the company reported normalized earnings of $0.57 per share in Q4 2025, it missed previous projections by $0.01. It also marked the fourth straight quarter, and the fifth out of the last six, when the company has missed its bottom-line target. Given the fact that there appears to be only one analyst that covers the stock, I would not put too much weight on these facts, but it is symptomatic of a company that seems to find a way to come up short of looking like a more solid investment option in a number of ways.

It will be interesting to see just how much the addition of Susquehanna adds to CZNC’s bottom line when it reports Q1 2026 earnings in about three weeks. The acquisition was projected to be roughly 17% accretive to Citizens & Northern’s FY 2026 earnings. Many of the usual performance metrics that I used to analyze a regional bank were impacted by merger costs in Q4 2025. Merger-related expenses in the last three months of last year totaled $6.9 million, a sizeable amount for a company that reported only $4.5 million in net income for that quarter.

When the performance of the bank is adjusted for these expenses, the picture does start to look a little better for CZNC. Adjusted earnings of $1.85 per share for FY 2025 were a 9.5% improvement from the $1.69 reported for FY 2024. The bank’s return on assets for FY 2025 of 0.85% rises to 1.09% when acquisition costs are removed. Return on equity expands from 7.87% to 10.00%. Even when adjusted, ROA and ROE ratios for the bank are below average, but they did show positive movement in the final quarter of last year. The full non-GAAP reconciliation for these figures is given below.

At the end of 2025, CZNC had a book value of $19.17 per share and a tangible book value of $15.11. At its current market price of $22.42, the stock trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.48, which is not exactly bargain territory. This ratio is right in the range of the average bank in the US. Considering that average includes a number of large-cap banks that trade at much higher valuations, I would expect a smaller regional bank like Citizens & Northern to have a lower multiple, given its performance over the last year. At the current time, the valuation of the stock is just not compelling for me until many of the performance ratios mentioned earlier improve.

What could help the bank improve its fortune in 2026? The first is continued improvement in net interest margin that could push more profits to the bottom of its income statement. CZNC started making progress here in FY 2025, as its NIM for FY 2025, 3.61%, was 31 basis points higher than in FY 2024.

Next would be better control of non-interest expenses. Even without the inclusion of merger costs, these costs rose 7.8% in FY 2025 to $80.0 million. In Q4 2025, non-interest expenses increased 25.7% from Q4 2024. Rising salaries and benefits as well as higher technology costs were largely to blame.

Risks to Consider

A few of the asset quality ratios for the bank are above the threshold that I like to see in the banks in which I feel most confident, and they are trending in the wrong direction. Nonperforming loans to total loans stood at 1.40% at the end of 2025, 16 basis points higher than where they started the year. Nonperforming assets and a share of total assets crossed over the 1% mark midway through 2025, finishing the year at 1.06%. The company’s allowance for credit losses is slightly above average at 1.32%. CZNC appears to have allocated enough cushion to cover most loan issues for now, but this is an area to watch in the first half of this year.

The bank’s high exposure to commercial real estate is partially reflected in those asset quality metrics. The table below shows greater detail for CZNC’s investor CRE that does not include multi-family properties. The highest shares of non-owner-occupied CRE belong to office and retail CRE. These two loan types have been in the spotlight over the last year as office properties have faced continued pressure from “work from home trends” and retail spaces have come under prolonged competition from online sellers. Office CRE may have hit a bottom from a valuation standpoint at the end of 2025, but recovery is uneven at best. Retail CRE continues to face pressure from trade conflicts, inflation, and consumer spending trends.

Of course, Citizens & Northern Corporation is subject to the usual risks that are associated with most small-cap regional banks. The odds of a rate cut from the Federal Reserve appear to be close to zero, at least for the first half of this year. At the same time, the odds of a recession in 2026 are getting closer to 50%. A pullback in economic activity in the US would impact most all banks, but small-cap institutions may be hit harder as they have a less diverse source of income and are more susceptible to loan defaults with less cushion than larger banks.

The Bottom Line

I was surprised to see that CZNC was rated so highly by Seeking Alpha’s quant analysis. The stock is currently ranked second out of 241 regional banks that are scored on the website. It is even more interesting to see that the company was considered a Sell by the same analysis just three months ago. Strong momentum from the stock in January is the biggest factor in boosting its overall grade. The “C-” in Growth and the “D” in Profitability are justified, in my opinion, and carry more weight with me when I think about my own outlook of the company's prospects.

I can see a scenario where income-oriented investors want to take a longer look at Citizens & Northern. The 5% yield is probably safe for now, barring any significant setback for the bank’s operations. With meaningful growth in its bottom line still yet to be seen, I expect CZNC to perform in line with most other small-cap regional banks for the first half of this year. This is my biggest reason for maintaining my hold rating on the stock.
